note: Vehicle selection retained after deleting the rejected generated guide batch. This file now captures researched roadside/driveway guide opportunities, not completed repair guides.

researchBasis.usedFor: Tire pressure, spare-tire readiness, TPMS, blowout safety, and tire-related crash risk.

researchBasis.source: AAA roadside assistance reporting via TIME
researchBasis.usedFor: Roadside-call reality check: tire damage and 12V battery issues dominate practical roadside assistance.
Reminder to check VIN/recall status before turning a known defect into a DIY repair.

roadsideGuideRules: A roadside guide must start with scene safety: get out of traffic, hazards on, visibility gear, passenger-side exit when needed, and call emergency services for crash, injury, fire, or unsafe shoulder.

The fix must require only common carried gear: phone, flashlight, gloves, reflective marker, tire gauge/inflator, spare/sealant kit, jump pack/cables, pliers, screwdriver, small socket/wrench set, fuses, tape, zip ties, water/coolant, washer fluid, and basic bulbs/blades.
The guide should get the driver to a safer place, home, or a shop. It should not pretend a roadside fix is a permanent repair.
Any fuel leak, brake hydraulic issue, steering loss, engine knock, oil-pressure warning, active fire risk, repeated stalling in traffic, or unsafe work location is a tow/shop decision.

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Low tire pressure or slow tread leak
driverCanDo: Read the door-jamb PSI and check all tires including the spare.
Inflate a low tire with a 12V compressor.
Use sealant or a plug kit only for a small tread puncture when the vehicle is safely parked.
Drive slowly to a tire shop and recheck pressure.
doNotDo: Do not plug sidewall damage.
Do not keep driving on a visibly flat tire.
Do not work beside fast traffic.
gear: Plug kit or OEM sealant kit

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Flat tire spare install or OEM inflator-kit use
roadsideProblemTypes.roadsideFit: excellent if the shoulder is safe
driverCanDo: Find the spare, jack points, wheel lock key, and lug wrench.
Chock the opposite wheel if available.
Loosen lugs before lifting, jack only at the approved point, install spare, and snug lugs in a star pattern.
Observe temporary spare speed and distance limits.
doNotDo: Do not change a traffic-side tire on a narrow shoulder.
Do not crawl under a vehicle supported only by its jack.
Do not use a temporary spare for normal driving.
gear: Spare or OEM tire repair kit

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Dead 12V battery, loose terminal, or left-lights-on discharge
driverCanDo: Confirm symptoms: clicks, dim lights, no crank, or accessories work but engine will not crank.
Inspect battery terminals for looseness or corrosion.
Tighten a loose terminal if the cable is accessible.
Jump-start with a portable jump pack or another vehicle using the owner-manual jump points.
doNotDo: Do not jump a cracked, frozen, leaking, swollen, or hot battery.
Do not guess on polarity.
Do not keep driving if the charging light remains on.

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Blown fuse for lights, power outlet, wipers, washer pump, or trailer lights
driverCanDo: Use the owner manual or fuse-box cover map.
Pull and inspect only the suspect fuse.
Replace with the same amperage rating.
Use the repair only to restore a safety-critical function long enough to get home or to a shop.
doNotDo: Do not install a higher-amp fuse.
Do not wrap foil around a fuse.
Do not keep replacing a fuse that blows again immediately.

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Visibility failure: wiper blade, washer fluid, headlight, brake light, or turn signal
driverCanDo: Replace a torn snap-on wiper blade.
Refill washer fluid and clear clogged nozzles if accessible.
Replace an accessible exterior bulb or restore it with the correct fuse.
Clean headlight, camera, radar, and windshield surfaces before continuing.
doNotDo: Do not drive in rain or darkness without working lights and wipers.
Do not disassemble bumper or sealed LED modules roadside.
Do not use plain water in freezing weather.

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Mild overheating or low coolant triage
driverCanDo: Turn off A/C, turn heat on if safe, and pull over.
Shut down and let the engine cool completely.
Check the coolant reservoir only when safe.
Top off the reservoir with premix coolant or water only as a temporary get-to-safety action.
doNotDo: Do not open a hot radiator or pressure cap.
Do not drive with the temperature gauge in the red.
Do not continue if coolant pours out, steam is visible, or the belt is missing.

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Dragging splash shield, loose liner, or hanging trim
driverCanDo: Stop before the part tears off or contacts the tire.
Secure loose plastic with zip ties or tape.
Remove only loose plastic that can be reached without crawling under the vehicle.
Drive slowly to a better repair location.
doNotDo: Do not reach near hot exhaust.
Do not work under a vehicle on a shoulder.
Do not touch brake, fuel, steering, or suspension parts.

roadsideProblemTypes.title: Light snow, mud, gravel, or ice traction recovery
roadsideProblemTypes.roadsideFit: good if not high-centered or in traffic
driverCanDo: Clear around tires and tailpipe.
Use traction mats, sand, kitty litter, or floor mats as a last resort.
Rock gently only if the owner manual allows it.
Stop before overheating the transmission or digging deeper.
doNotDo: Do not stand in front of or behind a spinning tire.
Do not use tow straps without rated recovery points.
Do not continue if the vehicle is high-centered, near a ditch, or unstable.

vehicles.vehicleNotes: Crossover audience benefits from tire-pressure, inflator, spare-location, and jump-start guidance more than engine-internal work.
Sedan guide should focus on no-crank triage, jump points, terminal access, and safe battery decisions.
Pickup-specific guide should cover under-bed spare lowering, wheel lock/key, heavy wheel handling, and trailer-light fuses.
Family-crossover roadside guide should emphasize passenger safety, shoulder choice, and spare/jack storage.
Guide should cover jump-start access and battery/terminal triage without implying throttle or heater-core work is roadside.
Modern crossover guide should verify whether the vehicle has a spare, run-flat, or sealant/inflator kit before giving tire steps.
This must be a triage-and-tow-aware guide, not a cooling-system repair guide. Steam, red gauge, fast coolant loss, or fire risk means stop and tow.
Large SUV guide should focus on vehicle stability while jacking, heavy-wheel handling, and when the shoulder is too unsafe.
Truck guide should cover spare lowering, safe jack placement, tire weight, trailer-light fuse checks, and gentle traction recovery.
Despite known purge-valve recalls, the roadside guide should prioritize tire and battery self-rescue plus VIN recall lookup for stalling.
Sedan guide should cover jump-starting, battery terminal access, and no-start triage before any part replacement claims.
Same practical guide family as Equinox: tire pressure, spare/inflator, jump-start, and coolant triage only.
Avoid engine-failure DIY. Good roadside content is visibility, jump-starting, tire inflation, and fuse checks.
Guide should cover tire changes, traction aids, and loose underbody trim, not TIPM or oil-cooler replacement.
Wrangler-specific roadside guidance can cover safe traction recovery and tire/spare use, with clear warnings against unsafe recovery points.
Avoid engine-replacement framing. Roadside guide should focus on visibility, battery, tire, and fuse self-rescue.
Family-SUV guide should prioritize passenger safety, spare/jack storage, and tow decisions over powertrain fixes.
Keep the roadside guide to battery/tire/visibility basics; CVT symptoms should route to tow/shop, not roadside repair.
Best fit is tire pressure/inflator/spare guidance. CVT complaints should be excluded from roadside DIY.
Truck guide should cover heavy wheel handling, spare lowering, trailer-light fuses, and traction recovery boundaries.
